text.title: predictive-analytics-pm Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for predictive-analytics-pm. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use a classical ML model (gradient boosting, Cox hazard, Prophet) for point forecasts and confidence intervals; use an LLM only for the plain-language analyst brief that accompanies the model output. Never use an LLM as the forecasting engine.
rule.rationale: LLMs asked "predict when this will finish" hallucinate confident dates without uncertainty quantification. Deterministic estimators produce calibrated probabilities that can be monitored for drift; LLM forecasts cannot be calibrated or monitored.

rule.statement: Deploy only a model that beats naive baselines (last-3-sprint velocity, mean cycle time) on a holdout set with statistical significance. Measure calibration with Brier score; reject any model with Brier score above 0.20.
rule.rationale: An uncalibrated model emitting "70% likely to slip" with no empirical basis is worse than intuition — it provides false confidence and cannot be improved without calibration data.

rule.statement: Retrain on a fixed monthly cadence AND on drift triggers (team composition change, process change, scope change). Track both; log every forecast with input snapshot for post-hoc audit.
rule.rationale: Concept drift makes yesterday's model stale within months of the first organizational change. Silent drift produces confident wrong forecasts that PM teams stop trusting — then stop using.

rule.statement: Require human approval for any action triggered by a forecast: resource reallocation, scope pull, escalation, or replanning. Agents emit recommendations only — never autonomous actions on forecast output.
rule.rationale: Auto-actioning a forecast creates feedback loops in the training data (the intervention changes the outcome, invalidating future calibration) and bypasses judgment calls that require organizational context.

rule.statement: Aggregate all analytics at team or program level; refuse to generate per-person productivity metrics from issue data.
rule.rationale: Per-person metrics from issue trackers are an HR and legal risk (GDPR, labor law, union agreements in some regions). They also incentivize gaming the data at the individual level, destroying the signal entirely.

rule.statement: Define the decision the forecast supports before training; if no decision changes with the forecast, do not build it.
rule.rationale: Forecasting without a decision context produces shelf-ware dashboards that stakeholders ignore.

rule.statement: Start with naive baselines (last-3-sprints velocity, mean cycle time) — only ship a model that beats them on holdout, with statistical significance.
rule.rationale: Naive baselines set the bar; a model that fails this test has no value to add.

rule.statement: Calibrate probabilistic outputs; report both the prediction and its reliability diagram each release.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders need to understand both the forecast and how much to trust it.

rule.statement: Always include feature attribution (SHAP) in stakeholder briefs; opaque scores destroy trust.
rule.rationale: PMs and executives need to understand the drivers behind a forecast to act on it with confidence.

rule.statement: Retrain on a fixed cadence (monthly) AND on drift triggers; track both.
rule.rationale: Regular retraining keeps the model synchronized with team evolution; drift triggers catch sudden changes.

rule.statement: Human-in-the-loop for any action — model recommends, human decides — especially for resource changes.
rule.rationale: Organizational decisions require context and judgment that models cannot provide.

rule.statement: Aggregate at team or program level, not per individual; prevents misuse for performance management.
rule.rationale: Individual metrics invite HR abuse and destroy psychological safety on teams.

rule.statement: Log every forecast with input snapshot for post-hoc audit; PM forecasts are revisited frequently.
rule.rationale: Forecasts improve when you track what you predicted, what happened, and why. Audit logs enable retrospectives.

rule.statement: Pair predictive output with value-stream-management flow metrics and dora-metrics for triangulation.
rule.rationale: Multiple signals strengthen forecasting confidence and catch model edge cases.

rule.statement: Document model assumptions in a model card; PMs and execs need to understand limits.
rule.rationale: Model cards prevent overconfidence and set realistic expectations for forecast reliability.

rule.statement: Use dvc for dataset and model version control, mlflow for experiment tracking and model registry.
rule.rationale: Reproducibility and version control prevent duplicate work and enable rollback on drift.
